---
title: "Working with <br>Factors"
subtitle: Day 12
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: ../img/horst-forcats.png
  data-background-size: 65%
  data-background-position: bottom right
  data-background-opacity: "0.8"
  data-slide-number: none
format: 
  revealjs:
    incremental: true
    code-copy: true
    code-link: true
    scrollable: true
auto-stretch: false
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)

library(countdown)
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(palmerpenguins)
library(patchwork)
library(ggthemes)
library(nycflights23)
library(here)
library(flextable)
slides_theme = theme_minimal(
  base_family = "Atkinson Hyperlegible",
  base_size = 16)

theme_set(slides_theme)
```


## Survivor `castaways` data

```{r}
#| echo: false
castaways = survivoR::castaways %>%
  mutate(
    version = as.factor(version),
    jury1 = as.numeric(jury),
    jury2 = as.factor(jury)
  )
castaways
```

## Both of these are categorical variables


```{r}
unique(castaways$version)
```

```{r}
unique(castaways$full_name) %>% head()
```
 
. . . 

### But are stored as different types 

```{r}
class(castaways$version)
```

```{r}
class(castaways$full_name) 
```

## Same graph, colored by the same categorical variable

```{r}
#| echo: false
p1 = ggplot(castaways %>% filter(version == "US"), aes(x = season, y = place, color = jury1)) + 
  geom_point()

p2 = ggplot(castaways %>% filter(version == "US"), aes(x = season, y = place, color = jury2)) + 
  geom_point()

p1 + p2
```


## Factors

R’s representation of categorical data. Consists of:

1. A set of **values**

1. An ordered set of valid **levels**

. . . 

```{r}
eyes <- factor(x = c("blue", "green", "green"), 
               levels = c("blue", "brown", "green"))
eyes
```


## Factors

Stored as an integer vector with a levels attribute

```{r}
unclass(eyes)
```

## {.center}

::::: columns
::: {.column .nonincremental .center width="40%"}

![](https://forcats.tidyverse.org/logo.png)
:::

:::{.column .nonincremental width="60%"}
- Simple functions for working with factors.

- Part of the `tidyverse`

```{r}
# loaded with tidyverse
library(forcats)
```

:::
:::::

## `gss_cat`

A sample of data from the General Social Survey, a long-running US survey conducted by NORC at the University of Chicago.

```{r echo=FALSE}
gss_cat
```

## Warm up

::: {.task .nonincremental}
Use `gss_cat` to answer the following questions.

1. Which religions watch the least TV?

2. Do married people watch more or less TV than single people?

::: 

```{r echo=FALSE}
countdown(4)
```


```{r}
#| label: gss-religion-tv
#| fig-width: 3
#| fig-height: 3.5
#| out-width: 100%
#| echo: false
#| eval: false
gss_cat %>%
  drop_na(tvhours) %>%
  group_by(relig) %>%
  summarize(tvhours = mean(tvhours)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(tvhours, relig)) +
    geom_point()
```

`r flipbookr::chunq_reveal("gss-religion-tv", font_size_code="70%", title = "Which religions watch the least TV?")`


## Which do you prefer?

```{r}
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 3.5
#| out-width: 100%
#| echo: false
p1 <- gss_cat %>%
  drop_na(tvhours) %>%
  group_by(relig) %>%
  summarize(tvhours = mean(tvhours)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(tvhours, relig)) +
    geom_point()

p2 <- gss_cat %>%
  drop_na(tvhours) %>%
  group_by(relig) %>%
  summarize(tvhours = mean(tvhours)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(tvhours, fct_reorder(relig, tvhours))) +
    geom_point() +
  labs(y = "relig")

p1 + p2
```

## Why is the y-axis in this order?

```{r}
#| fig-width: 4.5
#| fig-height: 3.5
#| out-width: 60%
#| echo: false

p1

```


## `levels()`

Use `levels()` to access a factor’s levels

```{r}
gss_cat %>% pull(relig) %>% levels()
```

## Most useful factor skills: {.center}


### 1. **Reorder** the levels

### 2. **Recode** the levels

### 3. **Collapse** levels

## `fct_reorder`

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
- `.f` factor vector
- `.x` variable to reorder by 
(in conjunction with `.fun`)
- `.fun` function to reorder by
- `.desc` put levels in descending order?
:::
::: {.column .nonincremental width="50%"}
```{r}
#| eval: false
fct_reorder(
  .f, 
  .x, 
  .fun = median, 
  ...,
  .desc = FALSE 
  )
```
:::
:::::

```{r reorder-tv-relig}
#| fig-width: 4.5
#| fig-height: 3.5
#| echo: false
#| eval: false


gss_cat %>%
  drop_na(tvhours) %>%
  group_by(relig) %>%
  summarize(tvhours = mean(tvhours)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(
    x = tvhours, 
    y = relig  #ROTATE
    y = fct_reorder(relig, tvhours)                 #ROTATE
  )) +
    geom_point()
```

`r flipbookr:::chunq_reveal("reorder-tv-relig", break_type = "rotate", widths = c(1.2,1), title = "Reorder relig by tvhours")`

## Try it

::: {.task .nonincremental}
Use `rincome_summary` to construct a dotplot of `rincome` against `age`.

Reorder `rincome` by `age`

::: 

```{r}
rincome_summary <- gss_cat %>%
  group_by(rincome) %>%
  summarize(
    age = mean(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    tvhours = mean(tvhours, na.rm = TRUE),
    n = n()
  )
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
countdown(2, 30)
```

## Which do you prefer? {.center}

```{r gss-age-marital}
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 3.5
#| out-width: 100%
#| echo: false

lp1 <- gss_cat %>%
  drop_na(age) %>%
  count(age, marital) %>%
  group_by(age) %>%
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(age, prop, color = marital)) +
    geom_line() +
  scale_color_colorblind("")

lp2 <- gss_cat %>%
  drop_na(age) %>%
  count(age, marital) %>%
  group_by(age) %>%
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(age, prop, 
    color = fct_reorder2(marital, age, prop))) +
    geom_line() +
  scale_color_colorblind("")

lp1 + lp2
```

## `fct_reorder2`

Reorders the levels of a factor by the Y values associated with the largest X values.


::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
- `.f` factor vector
- `.x` X variable
- `.y` Y variable
- `.fun` function to reorder by
- `.desc` put levels in descending order?
:::
::: {.column .nonincremental width="50%"}
```{r}
#| eval: false
fct_reorder2(
  .f, 
  .x, 
  .y, 
  .fun = median, 
  ...,
  .desc = FALSE
  )
```
:::
:::::

```{r}
#| label: reorder-marital-age
#| echo: false
#| eval: false
gss_cat %>%
  drop_na(age) %>%
  count(age, marital) %>%
  group_by(age) %>%
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ggplot(
    aes(age, 
        prop, 
        color = marital )) + #ROTATE
        color = fct_reorder2(marital, .x = age, .y = prop))) + #ROTATE
  
  geom_line() +
  scale_color_colorblind("")
```

`r flipbookr:::chunq_reveal("reorder-marital-age", title = "Reorder marital",  break_type = "rotate", lcolw = "40", rcolw = "60")`

```{r}
#| label: other-reordering-functions
#| echo: false
#| eval: false
gss_cat %>%
  ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(aes(x = marital))#ROTATE
  geom_bar(aes(x = fct_infreq(marital)))#ROTATE
  geom_bar(aes(x = fct_rev(fct_infreq(marital))))#ROTATE
```

`r flipbookr:::chunq_reveal("other-reordering-functions", title = "Other reordering functions",  break_type = "rotate", lcolw = "40", rcolw = "60")`

## Which political leaning watches more TV?

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-align: center
gss_cat %>%
   drop_na(tvhours) %>%
   group_by(partyid) %>%
   summarize(tvhours = mean(tvhours)) %>%
   ggplot(aes(tvhours, fct_reorder(partyid, tvhours))) +
     geom_point() +
     labs(y = "partyid")
```

How could we improve the `partyid` labels?
 
## `fct_recode`

Changes values of levels



::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
- `.f` factor vector
- `...` new level = old level pairs (as a named character vector)
:::
::: {.column .nonincremental width="50%"}
```{r}
#| eval: false
fct_recode(.f, ...)
```
:::
:::::

```{r}
#| label: fct-recode-partyid
#| echo: false
#| eval: false
gss_cat %>%
  drop_na(tvhours) %>%
  select(partyid, tvhours) %>%
    mutate(partyid = fct_recode(partyid,
    "Republican, strong"    = "Strong republican",
    "Republican, weak"      = "Not str republican",
    "Independent, near rep" = "Ind,near rep",
    "Independent, near dem" = "Ind,near dem",
    "Democrat, weak"        = "Not str democrat",
    "Democrat, strong"      = "Strong democrat")) %>% 
  group_by(partyid) %>%
  summarize(tvhours = mean(tvhours)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(tvhours, fct_reorder(partyid, tvhours))) +
  geom_point() + 
  labs(y = "partyid")
```

`r flipbookr:::chunq_reveal("fct-recode-partyid", title = "Recoding partyid",  lcolw = "40", rcolw = "60")`

## How can we combine these factor levels?

```{r}
#| echo: false
gss_cat %>%
  drop_na(tvhours) %>%
  select(partyid, tvhours) %>%
    mutate(partyid = fct_recode(partyid,
    "Republican, strong"    = "Strong republican",
    "Republican, weak"      = "Not str republican",
    "Independent, near rep" = "Ind,near rep",
    "Independent, near dem" = "Ind,near dem",
    "Democrat, weak"        = "Not str democrat",
    "Democrat, strong"      = "Strong democrat")) %>% 
  group_by(partyid) %>%
  summarize(tvhours = mean(tvhours)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(tvhours, fct_reorder(partyid, tvhours))) +
  geom_point() + 
  labs(y = "partyid") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(2, 3.6), 
                      clip = 'off') + 
  annotate("rect", xmin = 1.5, xmax = 2, ymin = 1.5, ymax = 4.5, col = "darkred", fill = "darkred", alpha = .3) + 
  annotate("rect", xmin = 1.5, xmax = 2, ymin = 5.5, ymax = 7.5, col = "darkblue", fill = "darkblue", alpha = .3) + 
  annotate("rect", xmin = 1.5, xmax = 2, ymin = 9.5, ymax = 10.5, col = "darkblue", fill = "darkblue", alpha = .3) 
```

## `fct_collapse()`

Changes multiple levels into single levels

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
- `.f` factor vector
- `...` named arguments set to a character vector (levels in the vector will be collapsed to the name of the argument)
:::
::: {.column .nonincremental width="50%"}
```{r eval=FALSE}
fct_collapse(.f, ...)
```
:::
:::::

```{r fct-collapse-partyid, eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
gss_cat %>%
  drop_na(tvhours) %>%
  select(partyid, tvhours) %>%
  mutate(
    partyid = 
      fct_collapse(
        partyid,
        conservative = c("Strong republican", 
                         "Not str republican", 
                         "Ind,near rep"),
        liberal = c("Strong democrat", 
                    "Not str democrat", 
                    "Ind,near dem"))
  ) %>% 
  group_by(partyid) %>%
  summarize(tvhours = mean(tvhours)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(tvhours, fct_reorder(partyid, tvhours))) +
  geom_point() + 
  labs(y = "partyid")
```

`r flipbookr:::chunq_reveal("fct-collapse-partyid", title = "Collapsing partyid",  lcolw = "40", rcolw = "60")`

```{r include=FALSE}
gss_cat <- gss_cat %>%
  drop_na(tvhours) %>%
  mutate(
    partyid = 
      fct_collapse(
        partyid,
        conservative = c("Strong republican", 
                         "Not str republican", 
                         "Ind,near rep"),
        liberal = c("Strong democrat", 
                    "Not str democrat", 
                    "Ind,near dem"))
  )
```

## Your turn


::: {.task .nonincremental}
Collapse the `marital` variable to have levels `Married`, `Not married`, and `No answer`

Include `"Never married"`, `"Divorced"`, and "`Widowed"` in `Not married`
:::

```{r echo=FALSE}
countdown(2)
```

## There are relatively few points in each of these groups

```{r}
#| echo: false


gss_cat %>%
  group_by(partyid) %>%
  summarize(tvhours = mean(tvhours)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(tvhours, fct_reorder(partyid, tvhours))) +
  geom_point() + 
  labs(y = "partyid") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(2, 3.6), 
                      clip = 'off') + 
  gghighlight::gghighlight(partyid %in% c("No answer", "Other party")) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = 1.6, xmax = 2, ymin = .5, ymax = 1.5, col = "darkgreen", fill = "darkgreen", alpha = .3) + 
  annotate("rect", xmin = 1.6, xmax = 2, ymin = 2.5, ymax = 3.5, col = "darkgreen", fill = "darkgreen", alpha = .3) + 
  annotate("rect", xmin = 1.6, xmax = 2, ymin = 5.5, ymax = 6.5, col = "darkgreen", fill = "darkgreen", alpha = .3)
  
```

## There are relatively few points in each of these groups

```{r echo=FALSE}
gss_cat %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = fct_infreq(partyid))) +
  geom_bar() +
  gghighlight::gghighlight(partyid %in% c("No answer", "Other party")) +
  labs(x = "partyid")
```

## `fct_lump()`

Collapses levels with fewest values into a single level. 

By default collapses as many levels as possible such that the new level is still the smallest.

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
- `f` factor vector
- `n` preserve the most common n levels
- `other_level = "Other"`
:::
::: {.column .nonincremental width="50%"}
```{r eval=FALSE}
fct_lump(
  f, 
  n, 
  other_level = "Other", 
  ...
)
```
:::
:::::


```{r fct-lump-partyid, eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
gss_cat %>%
  mutate(partyid = partyid) %>% #ROTATE
  mutate(partyid = fct_lump(partyid, n = 2)) %>% #ROTATE
  mutate(partyid = fct_lump(partyid, n = 3)) %>% #ROTATE
  ggplot(aes(partyid)) + 
  geom_bar() +
  labs(x = "partyid")
```

`r flipbookr:::chunq_reveal("fct-lump-partyid", title = "Lumping parytid",  break_type = "rotate", lcolw = "40", rcolw = "60")`

## Your turn: hotel bookings

::: {.task .nonincremental}
The remainder of the activity file has you fixing up some plots using hotel bookings data from Tidy Tuesday
:::

```{r}
#| echo: false
hotels <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/master/data/2020/2020-02-11/hotels.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

hotels %>%
  group_by(hotel, arrival_date_month) %>%   # group by hotel type and arrival month
  summarize(mean_adr = mean(adr)) %>%       # calculate mean adr for each group
  ggplot(aes(
    x = arrival_date_month,                 # x-axis = arrival_date_month
    y = mean_adr,                           # y-axis = mean_adr calculated above
    group = hotel,                          # group lines by hotel type
    color = hotel)                          # and color by hotel type
    ) +
  geom_line() +                             # use lines to represent data
  theme_minimal() +                         # use a minimal theme
  labs(
    x = "Arrival month",                    # customize labels
    y = "Mean ADR (average daily rate)",
    title = "Comparison of resort and city hotel prices across months",
    subtitle = "Resort hotel prices soar in the summer while ciry hotel prices remain relatively constant throughout the year",
    color = "Hotel type"
    )
```
